cards.front: What is a stack data structure?
cards.back: A container that follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) ordering. The last element added is the first one removed. Like a stack of plates.

cards.text: Stack operations: {{c1::push}} adds an element to the top, {{c2::pop}} removes the top element, and {{c3::top}} accesses the top element without removing it.

cards.back: Last In, First Out - ordering principle where the most recently added item is removed first

cards.text: To use std::vector as a stack: {{c1::push_back()}} to push, {{c2::pop_back()}} to pop, and {{c3::back()}} to access the top element.

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: First back() returns 3 (last pushed). After pop_back(), back() returns 2 (previous element).

cards.back: Nothing (void). It removes the last element but doesn't return it. Use back() to access the value before calling pop_back().

cards.explanation:
std::vector can efficiently implement stack behavior:
  • push_back() adds to end (top of stack)
  • pop_back() removes from end (top of stack)
  • back() accesses end element (peek at top)
  • All O(1) amortized time complexity
For dedicated stack, std::stack exists but uses std::deque by default.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: pop_back() returns void, not the element. Should use: int val = stack.back(); then stack.pop_back();
cards.explanation: You must call back() before pop_back() if you need the value.

cards.back: Returns a reference to the last element in the vector (the element at position size()-1). Does not remove it.

cards.text: To access the first element of a vector, use {{c1::front()}}. To access the last element, use {{c2::back()}}.

cards.question: Implement a safe pop that returns the value
cards.explanation: Check if empty, get value with back(), then pop_back(), return value.
